awards.title: Institutionalizing Undergraduate Research to Advance STEM Education and Student Outcomes at a Two-Year College
Institutionalizing Undergraduate Research to Advance STEM Education and Student Outcomes at a Two-Year College

papers.title: Reading Between the Party Lines: How Authoritarianism Correlates with Dystopian Literature Consumption and Political Affiliation
papers.abstract:
This study explored the relationship between authoritarianism and both the number of dystopian literature 
novels read and self-reported political affiliation. Our primary goal was to investigate literature's potential 
role in influencing authoritarian thinking and to determine how authoritarianism scores might differ across 
the political spectrum. We recruited 155 participants with an average age of 36.65 (SD = 15.97) from a 
Midwest community college's psychology classes and social media platforms. Participants were presented 
with 15 dystopian literature titles and asked to indicate how many they had read. They also provided their 
political affiliation and completed a measure of authoritarian thinking. We found a significant negative 
correlation between the total number of dystopian novels read and scores on authoritarian thinking, 
suggesting that as the number of dystopian books read increases, authoritarian thinking tends to decrease. 
Furthermore, the strength of this relationship varied depending on the specific literary work. Regarding 
political affiliation, individuals who self-identified as Republican had significantly higher authoritarian 
thinking scores compared to participants of all other political affiliations, shedding light on authoritarian 
divides across party lines. These findings have practical implications worth considering. As children, we 
are influenced by the ideologies of our parents, until we have the space to challenge our instilled beliefs 
through personal experiences. Many books included in this study have been subjected to book bans globally, 
and the observed negative correlation with authoritarian thinking is notable. Our research suggests a need 
to better understand the potential ideological impact of removing these kinds of books from educational 
curricula.

papers.title: No Difference in School Threat Assessment Regardless of Exposure to Violence, Emotional Intelligence, or Type of School Attended
papers.abstract:
There is sparse research exploring what allows or prevents individuals from detecting warning signs that school 
shooters commonly exhibit before their attack. Our study explored the role of exposure to violence, emotional 
intelligence (EI), and type of high school attended in an individual's ability to identify the common warning signs. We 
hypothesized that 1) high exposure to media with anti-social content is negatively correlated with identifying 
concerning behaviors, 2) the number of times violence is heard and/or witnessed at school is negatively correlated 
with identifying concerning behaviors, 3) EI is positively correlated with identifying concerning behaviors, and 4) 
type of high school attended (rural, urban, suburban) influences the ability to identify concerning behaviors. We 
developed three videos of actors demonstrating the warning signs typical of school shooters based on a form within 
the Comprehensive School Threat Assessment Guidelines. Participants were asked how confident they were that a 
target character in the video displayed various characteristics. We found no statistically significant results regarding 
our hypotheses. Nevertheless, we urge other researchers to pursue further techniques and hypotheses that could help 
our society better identify school shooters.

papers.title: Impostor Phenomenon and Identity Concealment: Role of Stigmas, Stereotypes, Academic Self-Concept, and Perfectionism in Community College Students
papers.abstract:
While community colleges are a critical access point for nearly half of all undergraduates, limited research 
exists on the effects of negative stereotypes on this population. Our goal was to examine the relationships 
between stereotypes, stigma, impostor phenomenon, identity concealment, academic self-concept, and 
perfectionism. Our sample consisted of 105 Midwestern community college students with an average age 
of 24.09 (SD = 9.67), who were predominantly women (79.50%) and white (65.70%). Our analysis found 
that internalization of negative stereotypes was positively correlated with impostor phenomenon and 
identity concealment. This suggests that as students internalize negative stereotypes about being a 
community college student, they become more likely to feel like an impostor or fraud and to hide their 
community college identity. Additionally, a student’s academic self-concept was found to be negatively 
correlated with the impostor phenomenon and identity concealment. This indicates that a stude

papers.title: Generational Differences in Entitlement, Intolerance, and Openness to New Experiences
papers.abstract:
This study empirically explored potential generational variations in entitlement, intolerance, and openness to new experiences across four cohorts: Baby Boomers, Generation X, Millennials, and Generation Z. Three 
hypotheses were tested: 1) Millennials and Generation Z would score higher on entitlement, 2) There would be differences in intolerance across cohorts, and 3) Older generations would score higher on openness. The study surveyed 348 participants from a community college in the Midwest. Established measures assessed entitlement, intolerance, and openness. Results showed Millennials scored significantly higher on entitlement than Baby Boomers and Generation X, while Generation Z had the highest levels overall. Also, Generation Z exhibited higher overall intolerance, particularly sexism, ageism, and religious intolerance, though Baby Boomers showed greater sexual orientation prejudice. Significant differences were found 
regarding openness; however, Millennials were most open to new experiences. Limitations of the current study include reliance on self-reports, potential biases from generational labels, and a homogeneous sample 
limiting generalizability. Despite these limitations, this study contributes empirical evidence on generational differences, highlighting the importance of nuanced, evidence-based understanding rather than popular media portrayals. By acknowledging complexities in generational analysis, further research can 
provide insights into how societal changes shape cohorts' values and worldviews.

papers.title: Exploring College Connectedness, Course Satisfaction, and Mental Well-Being
papers.abstract:
During the COVID-19 quarantine, colleges were forced to shift from in-person courses to asynchronous and synchronous online learning. Post-quarantine, many schools continued the trend of various learning modalities. The present study aimed to investigate how college connectedness (CC), course satisfaction, and mental well-being differ by modality and explore intrapersonal differences in the learning experience. In this study, 209 in-person or online community college students self-reported their CC with peers and faculty, course satisfaction, mental well-being, extraversion, and shyness. While past research (mainly at four-year institutions) found that course modality impacts student outcomes, we did not find differences; students experienced similar levels of CC, course satisfaction, and well-being, whether taking in–person or online classes. We did, however, find intrapersonal differences. Specifically, community college students with stronger well-being were more satisfied with their courses, students who were more extraverted were more connected to their peers, and students who were less shy were more connected to their peers and faculty. Additionally, we found that regardless of modality, students who feel more course satisfaction also 
feel more connected to their peers and faculty. This is important considering the college experience may change gradually and sometimes unexpectedly. By strengthening the mental well-being of students, there is potential to enhance course satisfaction and foster stronger connections among students, peers, and faculty. This, in turn, can fortify relationships and contribute to an improved student experience. The discussion includes potential reasons community college students exhibit similar levels of these important outcomes regardless of modality.

papers.title: Examining Influences on Beliefs About Psychology as a Science
papers.abstract:
This study explored how certain factors can influence people's beliefs in psychological myths and their views of psychology as a science. We assessed conservatism, religiosity, critical thinking, need for cognition, number of psychology courses taken, college credits earned, belief in psychology as a science, and endorsement of psychological myths. We hypothesized that conservatism and religiosity would correlate positively with myth endorsement and negatively with seeing psychology as a science, while 
critical thinking, need for cognition, psychology courses taken, and total credits would show negative correlations. Results revealed conservatism positively influenced myth endorsement and negatively influenced seeing psychology as a science, supporting the hypothesis. However, religiosity did not significantly correlate with either outcome, failing to support previous research and our hypothesis. As expected, critical thinking, need for cognition, psychology courses, and total credits were positively correlated to seeing psychology as a science and negatively related to myth endorsement. These findings highlight the role of conservative ideology, critical thinking, and educational attainment in shaping beliefs about psychological science and misconceptions. The discovery that religiosity did not impact these beliefs 
counters assumptions about the incompatibility between religion and science. Limitations like the homogenous sample and political scale must be addressed. Overall, this research bridges how people come to accept psychological misinformation with implications for public comprehension of mental health issues. We discuss how disseminating accurate psychological knowledge dismantles myths and how conservative distrust of science could negatively impact society if people become less likely to view psychology as 
legitimate or seek psychological help.

papers.title:
The Impact of Personality on College Student Connectedness, Course Satisfaction,
 and Mental Well-Being
papers.abstract:
The adaptability of college students during the academic paradigm shift caused by
 the COVID-19 pandemic is important to investigate in order to understand how we can
 encourage positive and healthy experiences for students. This study focused on analyzing
 the relationships between personality traits (extroversion, openness to experience,
 shyness) and college connectedness, course satisfaction, mental well-being, and desire
 to be more connected to college. Participants included 209 randomly selected students
 aged 18 years or older recruited from a community college in the Midwest. Participants
 completed a 150-item self-report survey in March 2022 that assessed the variables using
 standardized measures. As expected, correlational analyses found that extraversion had a
 significant positive relationship with college connectedness and mental well-being.
 However, we did not find significant relationships between extraversion and course
 satisfaction or desire to be more connected. Consistent with our hypothesis, openness
 had a significant positive correlation with each of the variables but was assessed
 cautiously due to a low alpha level. As expected, shyness had a significant negative
 correlation with college connectedness, course satisfaction, and mental
 well-being-being, but not with desire to be more connected. In studying personality as a
 static trait, we can better understand that extraverted, open, and shy individuals tend
 to maintain levels of college connectedness, satisfaction, and mental well-being even
 when unexpected turmoil (e.g., a pandemic) occurs. Future research can investigate how
 educators can adjust the culture of their courses to accommodate personality variation
 among students and develop personality-specific ways to strengthen the adaptability of
 college students.
